Introduction
Last time, I was reviewing an article written by my colleague and I read the following quote:
On the other hand, using the property with an arrow function incurs a small time and memory cost.
So I asked myself (and I also asked my colleague):
Oh! Really? Do we have any metrics to support that?
Here is a brief preview of our discussion:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

An illustration about our discussion with my colleague Christophe V. — That comic strip is generated by AI and I assume its defaults and its cringe.- Me: We should at least put a quick benchmark to illustrate and support that statement, right?
- Christophe V.: Yeah, good idea. But it overwhelms the scope of the article 😕
- Christophe V.: And I don’t feel comfortable enough with JS/TS to profile it without doing some research on how to do it. That will take some time and delay the article’s publication 😕
- Me: Well, I know quite well JS/TS and I know how to profile it 😐
So he decided that I should write an article about it. And here we are 🙂
Method vs Function as property in JS/TS classes — The Code
So, in order to profile the difference between methods and functions as properties in JavaScript / TypeScript classes, we should start with some code.
We will have two files. Two implementations that do exactly the same thing. Just one with methods in classes and the other with functions as properties in classes.

• object-method-bench.js :

• function-property-bench.js :
What are these files doing?

• Declare 3 classes: LineProcessor, ResultsComputer and UnderTest.

• Create a new instance of UnderTest and immediately call its main method.
Yes, and so?
Ok, Ok. Here is the global idea: Read an input file where each line contains a simple mathematical operation between 2 operands and sum the results of those operations and finally print the total to the console.
In order to profile something and observe if there is a significant difference, we will have to perform tests on a large set of data. We should have the creation of lots of class instances and various methods / functions as properties calls.
That is what is done here. These implementations do not claim to be the most efficient but are here to illustrate what we are trying to observe and prove:
Is there really a performance or CPU / Memory footprint difference between methods and functions as properties in JavaScript / TypeScript classes?
JavaScript / TypeScript — How to profile?
We could perform a quick search with our favorite tool: ”How to profile a JavaScript/TypeScript application?”
As we are talking about the JavaScript ecosystem, it will almost certainly list many libraries and tools on NPM, even some that claim to be “fast” because they are written in Rust 🙃
Yes, BUT: I may be “old-fashioned” but we will first take a look at the official documentation.
There is a section “Profiling Node.js applications” in the Getting Started section.
TL;DR; -> Node.js has a built-in profiler. No need to install some dependencies to start 🙂
AND: If we take a look at the JavaScript language specifications, we can find that there is a complete “Performance” API 😯. This means that we could profile JavaScript / TypeScript code in a way that is completely runtime-agnostic?!?
Well, it seems like a good starting point 🙂
JavaScript / TypeScript — The Profiling
So here we are:

• We have the question we are trying to answer

• We have a benchmark scenario

• We have the comparison elements

• We have the tools to observe what is going on during the execution
Let’s do this!
Here is the setup I am using to do the experiment:

• A professional laptop (a 16-inch 2024 MacBook Pro)

• A terminal (Warp)

• An IDE (VS Code)

• NVM with Node.js lts/krypton (24) used for the project
I have an input file with a hundred lines that I generated (with a basic JavaScript script or with a quick LLM prompt).
Let’s start by following the Node.js documentation:
First file execution:
$> NODE_ENV=production node --prof object-method-bench.jsFinal Result: 6364359
Second file execution:
$> NODE_ENV=production node --prof function-property-bench.jsFinal Result: 6364359
Once we have done that, we should see two new files created in the current directory (named isolate-0xnnn-v8.log)
Let’s take a look at their content 🤓:

...😳 Well, let’s be honest, it is not human-friendly nor fun at all!
These files contain thousands of lines and those lines describe “tick” instructions that occurred during the execution of the Node.js program.
The documentation describes that and tells us to use the tick processor bundled with Node.js to make sense of these files 🙂
$> node --prof-process isolate-0x111-v8.log > object-method-bench-processed.txt$> node --prof-process isolate-0x222-v8.log > function-property-bench-processed.txt
Let’s take a look at their content 🤓:

Well, it seems a little bit easier to read and understand, but let’s be honest: it is not fun at all!
These files show information by section and by language. There is a section “summary” that gives us the distribution of what the program has used.
Now that we have these files, we can start to analyze them and try to find some differences between the two implementations. Maybe we can use some tools to do that for us? Let’s give an LLM a try, as it seems to be the new silver bullet for everything 🫠
Here is the prompt I used to ask the LLM (Github Copilot with Claude Sonnet 4.6 model) to analyze these files and give me some insights about the differences between the two implementations:
Can you analyze these Node.js profiling files and point out the key differences?And here are the results:
## What's being compared
| Bench | Pattern |
|-------|---------|
| `object-method-bench.js` | Prototype methods + `.bind(this)` |
| `function-property-bench.js` | Arrow function class fields (per-instance) |## Key profiling differences
### 1. Garbage Collection
| | GC ticks |
|-------|---------|
| Object method | **1 tick (2.1%)** |
| Function property | **0 ticks (0.0%)** |
Counterintuitive: arrow function class fields create one function object per instance, so you'd expect more GC pressure. The single GC tick in the method version may be linked to the .bind(this) call in promiseWorkerBuilder — bind() allocates a new bound function object on every processLine() call.
### 2. Unaccounted ticks
| | Unaccounted |
|-------|---------|
| Object method | 2 ticks (4.2%) |
| Function property | **5 ticks (10.2%)** |
Higher unaccounted ticks often indicate code that V8 has heavily optimized or inlined, making it invisible to the statistical profiler. This slightly favors the function-property approach at runtime.
### 3. Top C++ hotspot share
Both profiles are dominated by `std::__1::__shared_ptr_emplace<node::SocketAddress>::~__shared_ptr_emplace()` (Node.js socket/stream initialization), but it takes a **larger** slice in the method bench:
| | Share of total ticks |
|-------|---------|
| Object method | **39.6%** |
| Function property | **28.6%** |
### 4. System call differences
- Object method bench hits `_pthread_key_create` + `___pthread_init`
- Function property benc
